perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agent s who need to have access to the Co n- fidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the co n- fidential quality of the information provide d and will ensure that their agreement is o b- tained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained he rein will be s trictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient will endeavor not to disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who may need to have access to the Confidential Information. The Recipient will generally inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will try to ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on similar terms as set forth in this Agreement. The Recipient will be somewhat responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein are observed and will assume some liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will not disclose' with 'will endeavor not to disclose,' 'need to have access' with 'may need to have access,' 'will inform' with 'will generally inform,' 'will ensure' with 'will try to ensure,' 'will be responsible' with 'will be somewhat responsible,' and 'full liability' with 'some liability.' These changes weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality, creating uncertainty regarding the recipient's responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the NDA's protective measures for trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The 'endeavor,' 'generally inform,' 'try to ensure,' and 'somewhat responsible' weaken the commitment to confidentiality and liability, potentially rendering the protective measures insufficient under the Act, especially considering the need to prevent misappropriation.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1836 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1836 : Civil proceedings Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date §1836. Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes strict obligations on the recipient regarding confidentiality and liability. The changed text weakens these obligations by introducing ambiguity, using terms like 'endeavor,' 'generally inform,' 'try to ensure,' 'somewhat responsible,' and 'some liability.' This contradicts the need for 'reasonable measures to protect trade secrets' as required by laws such as the Trade Secrets Act. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 (18 USC 1836) discusses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, implying a need for robust protective measures, which the modified text undermines by creating ambiguities. Therefore, the changes create a legal contradiction as they weaken the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement and potentially fail to provide sufficient protection for trade secrets, making the contract less compliant with the intent of trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 The Recipient shall treat all Confidential Information with the same de gree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 The Recipient should consider treating all Confidential Information with a degree of care similar to that which it generally uses for its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Recipient to treat Confidential Information with the same degree of care as their own. The modified text weakens this obligation by using 'should consider' instead of 'shall,' 'a degree of care similar to that which it generally uses' instead of 'the same degree of care,' which are both more subjective and less stringent. This introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause aims to provide a level of protection equivalent to the Recipient's own confidential information. The modified language introduces ambiguity regarding the level of care required, which could fail to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The 'should consider' and 'generally uses' phrases could be interpreted as not requiring concrete or consistent actions, potentially leading to insufficient protection under the law.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain secrecy as indicated by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, supporting the idea that there's a legal framework for protecting trade secrets, and the modified clause, by using ambiguous language like 'should consider' and 'generally uses,' may not provide sufficient protection under this framework. The change from 'shall' to 'should consider' is a significant weakening of the obligation, making it less enforceable and more open to interpretation, thereby contradicting the intent to provide a certain level of protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall pr omptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form repr o- duced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information di s- closed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contempla ted in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may r equest it to the Recipient .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall endeavor to return or destroy all accessible copies, including reasonably identifiable notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient, provided that the Recipient deems it feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The phrase 'endeavor to return or destroy' makes the action less mandatory. The term 'accessible copies' limits the scope of what needs to be returned or destroyed, potentially leaving some copies unaccounted for. The addition of 'reasonably identifiable notes' adds a subjective element. Finally, 'provided that the Recipient deems it feasible' gives the Recipient discretion to not comply, creating significant uncertainty about the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The weakening of the return/destroy obligation could conflict with the Economic Espionage Act, which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. If the recipient does not make a strong effort to return/destroy the documents, or if they determine that it is not feasible, and this results in a trade secret being stolen or disclosed, then the weakened language of the NDA may make it difficult to enforce because 'endeavor,' 'accessible copies', 'reasonably identifiable notes,' and 'deems it feasible' all introduce significant loopholes. The modifications undermine the reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction with the spirit of laws like the Economic Espionage Act, which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation by requiring 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity, using language like 'endeavor to return or destroy,' limiting the scope to 'accessible copies' and 'reasonably identifiable notes,' and adding a feasibility clause: 'provided that the Recipient deems it feasible.' These modifications introduce loopholes that could undermine the protection of trade secrets, thereby creating a legal contradiction, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have ju risdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement may be construed and interpreted according to principles arguably derived from the laws of any jurisdiction which the parties may later agree is relevant. The determination of the appropriate court for resolving disputes shall be subject to mutual agreement at the time a dispute arises.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly defines the applicable law and jurisdiction. The modified text introduces several ambiguities. 'May be construed and interpreted' weakens the binding nature of the law. 'Principles arguably derived from the laws of any jurisdiction which the parties may later agree is relevant' makes the applicable law uncertain and subject to change. 'Subject to mutual agreement at the time a dispute arises' makes the jurisdiction uncertain as well.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications concerning jurisdiction could contradict 28 U.S. Code § 1391, which establishes rules for determining proper venue in federal court. By stating that the court will be 'subject to mutual agreement at the time a dispute arises,' the modified text attempts to circumvent established legal procedures for determining venue. This creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes over which court has jurisdiction, potentially conflicting with the statute's aim to provide clear guidelines for venue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 28 U.S. Code § 1391 regarding venue. The original contract specifies a jurisdiction, but the modified text makes the determination of the appropriate court 'subject to mutual agreement at the time a dispute arises.' This directly opposes the purpose of 28 U.S. Code § 1391, which is to provide clear guidelines for determining venue in federal court. The law snippet states that 'this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States.' By deferring the venue decision to a later agreement, the modified contract attempts to bypass the statute's established rules.
